text: Good morning, my name is Cataldo Doria and I am the Medical Director of the Cancer Center and the Robotic Institute at Capital Health. Capital Health is a beautiful organization in central New Jersey.
Today I would like to show you the way I perform a distal pancreatectomy with the splenectomy.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the gastrocolic ligament, providing access to the lesser sac. The instrument safely transects the short gastric vessels during this initial step of the operation.
The surgeon approaches the tissue between the spleen and the stomach, nearing completion of this section of the operation.
And in my mind, it's always very safe to proceed with the vessel sealer.
It takes a little bit longer time, but it gives a very good control of bleeding.
The surgeon dissects the splenic artery near its origin from the celiac trunk, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
Not everyone likes to use the hook, but I find it very useful and I think this section comes out very nicely.
The surgeon prepares to clip the splenic artery, placing two clips on the proximal end and one on the distal end for enhanced safety.
I'm sure many of my colleagues use just two clips, but I think it makes me sleep better when I leave two clips on the proximal end of the splenic artery.
The surgeon divides the splenic artery using scissors and begins developing the retropancreatic tunnel to facilitate the division of the pancreatic parenchyma.
The surgeon dissects the inferior border of the pancreas, progressing towards the superior border to locate the splenic vein, visible as a bluish area, using a hook for dissection.
The surgeon is around the splenic vein, which is about to be clipped. Several collateral veins have been divided between clips.
The surgeon places one clip proximally and two clips distally on the splenic artery, then divides the splenic vein using scissors.
The surgeon reveals the retropancreatic tunnel beneath the gland, providing a clear view of the space created for further surgical manipulation.
Sometimes it's easier to divide the gland before the vein, but in this particular case, I thought it was easier to go the other way around.
The surgeon has completely divided the splenic vein, which is now visibly separated.
The surgeon completes the creation of the tunnel beneath the pancreas, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
That opening always looks big enough but it's never big enough so I tend to make it slightly bigger than what I think is okay and I feel always safer to have an umbilical tape

The surgeon uses umbilical tape to elevate the gland, facilitating the safe insertion of a mechanical stapler. A clip is placed on the tape to prevent slipping during manipulation. The stapler, equipped with guards, is introduced into the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a mechanical stapler with guards to divide the pancreatic parenchyma, ensuring protection against leaks during the firing of the stapler.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide tissue on the posterior aspect of the pancreas, as well as on the upper and lower aspects of the gland, ensuring clear separation of these areas.
The surgeon completes the tissue division around the pancreas using a vessel sealer, ensuring precise separation of the gland's posterior and lateral aspects.
It's a very nice dissection, relatively easy.
The surgeon uses the vessel sealer without activating energy to manipulate tissue around the pancreas, demonstrating a technique visible in this part of the video.
The spleen appears completely devascularized, with no visible blood supply, indicating that the operation is nearing completion.
The surgeon takes the final bites of tissue anchoring the spleen to the retroperitoneum, completing the division of the specimen, which is now ready for extraction.
